questions.question: What does CQRS stand for, and what is its core idea?
options: Command Query Responsibility Segregation — use a different model to update data (commands) than the one used to read data (queries)
Concurrent Query Replication Strategy — replicate queries across read replicas for throughput
Command Queue Routing Service — route all writes through a durable message queue
Centralized Query Resolution System — funnel all reads and writes through one unified model
questions.explanation: CQRS = Command Query Responsibility Segregation: separate the model used to update data from the model used to read it. The other options invent unrelated expansions and contradict the 'separate models' premise.

questions.question: CQRS builds on Bertrand Meyer's Command Query Separation (CQS). What does CQS say about an individual method?
options: A method is either a command (changes state, returns nothing) or a query (returns data, no side effects) — never both
Every method must return a status code so callers can detect failure
A method should accept either a command object or a query object but never both as parameters
Commands must be asynchronous and queries must be synchronous
questions.explanation: CQS classifies each method as a command (mutates state, returns nothing) or a query (returns data, no side effects). CQRS lifts that distinction from the method level to the model level. The other options describe rules CQS does not make.

questions.question: CQRS is essentially CQS applied at the model level rather than the method level.
questions.explanation: The reviewer states CQRS 'lifts CQS from the method level up to the model level': separate command and query models instead of one CRUD model serving both.

questions.question: How should CQRS be scoped within a system?
options: As a component-level pattern applied inside a single bounded context, not a top-level architecture for the whole system
As the overall architectural style governing every service in the system
As a database-only concern that the application layer never sees
As a UI rendering technique applied uniformly to every screen
questions.explanation: The reviewer is explicit: CQRS is a pattern applied inside a bounded context/microservice, not an architectural style for the whole system — most of a system is fine as CRUD.

questions.question: According to the commands-vs-queries contrast, what does the query (read) side contain?
options: DTOs/projections shaped for the screen, with no domain logic — just read and map
The full set of domain aggregates and invariants used for validation
Business tasks like 'Book room' that change state
Transactional integrity guarantees enforced across multiple aggregates
questions.explanation: The read side returns DTOs/projections optimized for presentation and deliberately carries no domain logic. Validation, domain logic, and aggregate consistency belong to the command (write) side.

questions.question: Adopting CQRS necessarily means having two separate databases, one for writes and one for reads.
questions.explanation: False. The basic form uses one shared store and only separates the models; two stores is the advanced form. The reviewer lists 'does not require two databases' as a common myth.

questions.question: In the advanced form with separate read and write stores synced asynchronously, what is the main consequence?
options: Eventual consistency — the read store can briefly lag the write store, so reads may show stale data
Strong consistency — reads always reflect the latest committed write immediately
Loss of durability — committed writes can be silently dropped during sync
Write amplification that makes the command side reject concurrent commands
questions.explanation: Async sync from write store to read store introduces eventual consistency: reads can briefly lag writes. Strong consistency is precisely what you give up, and the pattern does not imply lost durability or rejected commands.

questions.question: Which of the following are situations the reviewer cites as good fits for CQRS? (Select all that apply.)
options: Collaborative domains where many users concurrently read/modify the same data
Disparate read/write scaling where reads vastly outnumber writes
Task-based UIs / complex domains with a rich write model and a logic-free read model
Simple CRUD domains where reads and writes have the same shape
A desire to apply one uniform model across the entire system
questions.explanation: Collaborative domains, disparate read/write scaling, and task-based/complex domains are listed as good fits. Simple CRUD and system-wide uniform application are exactly where the reviewer warns against CQRS.

questions.question: What is Martin Fowler's overall caution about CQRS, per the reviewer?
options: For most systems it adds risky complexity and is difficult to use well; it often becomes a drag on productivity
It should be the default starting point for every new greenfield system
It is mainly a performance anti-pattern that should never be used in production
It is harmless boilerplate that adds no real cost in any scenario
questions.explanation: Fowler warns that for most systems CQRS adds risky complexity, is difficult to use well, and frequently becomes a significant drag on productivity. The other options misstate his stance in both directions.

questions.question: What is the correct relationship between CQRS and Event Sourcing?
options: They are independent patterns, frequently combined, but neither requires the other
Event Sourcing is a strict prerequisite for any CQRS implementation
CQRS is just a synonym for Event Sourcing
Using CQRS automatically means you are storing events instead of current state
questions.explanation: The reviewer states they are independent patterns often paired but neither requires the other. CQRS does not imply storing events, and Event Sourcing is not a prerequisite.

questions.question: Messaging (a message bus/queue) is part of the definition of CQRS and is required to implement it.
questions.explanation: False. Messaging is an optional, common companion for processing commands or publishing events, but it is not part of the CQRS definition. When used, you must handle failures, duplicates, and retries.

questions.question: How does an in-process mediator (e.g., MediatR in .NET) relate to CQRS?
options: It is a common implementation vehicle for routing commands/queries to handlers, but CQRS doesn't require a mediator and a mediator doesn't imply CQRS
A mediator is mandatory; without one you cannot separate read and write models
Using a mediator automatically makes any codebase a CQRS system
A mediator replaces the need for separate command and query models
questions.explanation: A mediator is a convenient way to route requests to handlers, but the relationship is orthogonal: CQRS does not require a mediator, and a mediator does not imply CQRS. It certainly does not eliminate the need for separate models.

questions.question: In the 'simplified CQRS' .NET approach described, how are writes and reads typically handled?
options: Writes go through the EF Core domain model; reads use lightweight queries (e.g., Dapper) returning DTOs — against a single database
Writes and reads both use heavyweight EF Core change-tracked entities against two separate databases
Writes use raw Dapper while reads use the full EF Core domain aggregates
Both sides bypass any ORM and issue hand-written SQL against an event store
questions.explanation: Simplified CQRS keeps a single database: writes go through the EF Core domain model while reads use lightweight queries (e.g., Dapper) returning DTOs. The other options invert or complicate this arrangement.

questions.question: On the command (write) side, how should a command best be modeled in CQRS?
options: As a business task such as `BookRoom`, capturing intent, rather than a field-level CRUD update
As a generic `UpdateEntity(fields)` setter that mirrors the database row
As a query that returns the resulting entity so the UI can re-render
As a projection DTO shaped exactly for the screen that issued it
questions.explanation: Commands model business tasks (intent) like 'Book room', not field-level updates. A generic field setter is the CRUD model CQRS moves away from; returning data or shaping a screen DTO are query-side concerns.

questions.question: How does CQRS interact with Domain-Driven Design, per the reviewer?
options: The write side is where DDD aggregates and invariants pay off, while the read side deliberately skips them
DDD aggregates must be enforced identically on both the read and write sides
The read side is where DDD invariants are validated before data reaches the write store
CQRS forbids using DDD aggregates anywhere in the system
questions.explanation: DDD aggregates and invariants live on the write side; the read side intentionally skips them and just maps to DTOs. Enforcing invariants on the read side, or banning DDD entirely, both contradict the reviewer.

questions.question: Which statements about CQRS costs and myths are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: Eventual consistency is a real cost only once the read and write stores are separated
The basic single-store form separates the models but avoids cross-store eventual consistency
When messaging is used, you must handle failures, duplicates, and retries
Basic single-store CQRS still incurs eventual consistency between its two models
CQRS is required to be applied across the entire system to be valid
questions.explanation: Eventual consistency arises only when stores are separated, so the single-store form avoids it, and messaging brings failure/duplicate/retry handling. The basic form does not incur cross-store lag, and CQRS is a targeted pattern — not a system-wide mandate.

questions.question: Under strict Command Query Separation, which aspect of this method violates CQS?
options: It both mutates state (`_balance -= amount`) and returns data, so it is neither a pure command nor a pure query
It uses `decimal` instead of `double`, which CQS prohibits
It is a query because it returns a value, so it fully satisfies CQS
It is a command because it has a side effect, so it fully satisfies CQS
questions.explanation: CQS requires a method to be either a command (mutates, returns nothing) or a query (returns, no side effects) — never both. This method does both, violating CQS; the parameter type is irrelevant, and it is not a 'pure' command or query precisely because it mixes the two.

questions.question: Given this CQRS query handler, what is wrong with it relative to the read-side contract?
options: A query must have no side effects and carry no domain logic — applying a discount and saving belong on the command side
Nothing is wrong; query handlers are expected to enforce domain invariants before returning DTOs
The only issue is that it returns a DTO instead of the domain aggregate
It should load from the write store rather than mapping to a DTO at all
questions.explanation: The read side returns DTOs with no domain logic and no side effects; mutating the aggregate and saving is command-side behavior leaking into a query. Returning a DTO is exactly correct, so the other options misidentify the fault.
